attributes.stem: A four-hour-old boy presents with rapid breathing in the postnatal ward. He was born at 37 weeks gestation via spontaneous vaginal delivery. The pregnancy was uncomplicated. On examination, he appears unwell with a pulse rate of 180/min, respiratory rate 80/min, temperature 38.2°C, and oxygen saturation 92% on room air. He has intercostal recession and nasal flaring. Chest auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. Blood cultures reveal gram positive cocci in chains.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This neonate presents with features of early-onset sepsis within hours of birth. The clinical presentation and gram stain findings strongly suggest Group B Streptococcus sepsis. The presentation with respiratory distress, fever, and tachycardia within the first 24 hours of life is classic for early-onset GBS disease.

Staphylococcus aureus sepsis would typically show gram-positive cocci in clusters rather than chains on gram stain. While it can cause early-onset sepsis, it is less common than GBS in this setting.

Streptococcus pneumoniae sepsis is rare in the early neonatal period. While it also appears as gram-positive diplococci in chains on gram stain, it is characteristically lancet-shaped and tends to occur in pairs. When it occurs, it is usually associated with premature rupture of membranes and chorioamnionitis, which are not present in this case.

Enterococcus faecalis sepsis, while possible in neonates, is less common than GBS. It appears as gram-positive cocci that can occur singly, in pairs, or in short chains. It typically occurs in premature infants or those with other risk factors such as central lines or previous antibiotic exposure.

Listeria monocytogenes sepsis would appear as gram-positive rods rather than cocci on gram stain. While it is an important cause of early-onset neonatal sepsis, the gram stain appearance excludes this diagnosis.

The presence of gram-positive cocci in chains on gram stain, combined with the timing of presentation and clinical features make Group B Streptococcus the most likely causative organism.
learningPoints: Early-onset Group B Streptococcus infection typically presents within the first 24-48 hours of life with respiratory distress.
Risk factors for early-onset GBS disease include maternal GBS colonisation, prematurity, and inadequate intrapartum prophylaxis.
Gram stain showing gram-positive cocci in chains is characteristic of Streptococcus species.
references: Madrid L, Seale AC, Kohli-Lynch M, et al. Infant Group B Streptococcal Disease Incidence and Serotypes Worldwide: Systematic Review and Meta-analyses. Clin Infect Dis. 2017;65(suppl_2):S160-S172. doi:10.1093/cid/cix656
